title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concept: MLE calculates N-gram probabilities by directly counting their frequency in the dataset, but fails on sequences that never appeared in training.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of an N-gram language model?

options.text: To translate text into different languages
To predict the next word in a sequence
To check if a word is spelled correctly

exercises.explain: An N-gram model predicts the next word in a sequence based on the words that came right before it.

cards.title: Guessing the Next Word
cards.body: How does an AI decide which word should come next? It starts by looking at the past! By counting what words usually follow others in its training data, it makes a calculated guess. Let's look at the simplest way to do this.

cards.title: What is MLE?
cards.body: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) is a method where we calculate the probability of a word sequence by counting how often it appears in our training data. We divide the count of a sequence by the count of the words that came before it.

cards.title: The Ice Cream Tracker
cards.body: Imagine you want to know what someone's favorite ice cream is. You watch them buy ice cream 10 times. If they pick chocolate 8 times, you guess there is an 80% chance they will choose chocolate next time. You are estimating based only on what you saw!

cards.title: Counting in Action
cards.body:
Suppose your training text is: "read books, write books, love books". 
The word "books" appears 3 times. "read books" appears 1 time. 
Using MLE, the probability of "books" following "read" is 1 divided by 1, which is 100%!

cards.title: The Zero Trap
cards.body: People think that if a phrase has a 0% probability in MLE, it just means it is rare. In reality, a 0% probability acts like a strict wall: the AI will completely block that phrase from ever being generated, even if it makes perfect sense!

cards.title: Seen vs. Unseen
cards.body: See how MLE handles words it has seen versus words it has never encountered before in its training text.

exercises.prompt: How does Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) find the probability of N-grams?

options.text: By asking human editors to rate word choices
By counting how often word sequences appear in a dataset
By translating the words into another language first
By picking random words from a dictionary

exercises.explain: MLE is simple: it calculates probabilities by dividing the number of times a sequence appears by the total occurrences of its starting words.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a word pair never appears in the training dataset, MLE will assign it a probability of zero.
exercises.explain: This is true. If a sequence is missing from the training data, its count is 0, so MLE assigns it a probability of 0.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of MLE's limitation.
exercises.sentence: Because MLE relies strictly on raw text counts, it fails on sequences that never appeared in the training data.
exercises.explain: MLE relies completely on historical counts. If a word combination wasn't counted during training, the model assumes it can never happen.

exercises.prompt:
An AI is trained on a small dataset where "yellow banana" appears often, but "yellow zebra" never appears. Which output was generated by a strict MLE model?

Output A: "I saw a yellow zebra standing by the tree."
Output B: "I saw a yellow banana standing by the tree."
exercises.explain: Output B is the result of MLE. Since "yellow zebra" has a count of 0 in the tiny training set, MLE assigns it a 0% probability, making it impossible to generate.

exercises.prompt: You train an N-gram model using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). If the phrase "blue orange" never appears in your training books, how does MLE handle this phrase when generating text?

options.text: It guesses a small random number.
It shares the probability of 'blue' evenly.
It asks the user to type it again.

exercises.explain: MLE relies entirely on what was observed. Since the phrase "blue orange" was never seen in the dataset, MLE calculates its frequency as 0, assigning it a 0% probability.
